text: The video shows a large abdominal wall defect with a 5mm VisiPort inserted at the umbilicus to establish pneumoperitoneum. Dense honeycomb adhesions are encountered, and the surgeon uses the camera to gently create space for a second working port. Extensive division of adhesions is performed using both blunt and sharp dissection to restore normal anatomy, avoiding the central hernia defect. Additional working ports are inserted, and the anatomy becomes clearer, revealing a view from the left side towards the splenic flexure and the extensive midline hernia, with the left colon visible.

The surgeon visualizes the descending colon and begins lateral mobilization of the left colon, with the patient positioned left side up. Working ports are placed from the left upper to lower quadrant. The mobilization extends to the splenic flexure, with division of dense medial omental adhesions. At the pelvic brim, extensive inflammatory adhesions are carefully dissected away from retroperitoneal structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects at the pelvic brim, avoiding retroperitoneal structures like the ureter due to previous inflammation. Extensive small bowel adhesions to the sigmoid colon and its mesentery are visible, requiring meticulous separation to facilitate medial vascular dissection. Once space is established, the surgeon begins the medial dissection and high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, performing skeletonization of the vascular pedicle despite challenging port positioning.

The surgeon uses an energy device to assist in the precise skeletonization of the vascular pedicle. Retroperitoneal structures are carefully identified and dissected away from the sigmoid colon. A left lower quadrant incision is made for specimen extraction.

The surgeon performs an en bloc resection of the colon with adherent small bowel, associated with local tumor perforation. A primary stapled colorectal anastomosis and a side-to-side stapled small bowel anastomosis are completed without a defunctioning ileostomy.
